bossBattles.name: The OSI Titan
bossBattles.description: A towering entity of 7 layers. Each wrong answer collapses a layer.

questions.question: Which OSI layer is responsible for end-to-end error recovery and flow control?
questions.explanation: Layer 4 (Transport) provides end-to-end communication, error recovery via TCP, and flow control using windowing.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q41; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 2: OSI Model

questions.question: Which OSI layer sends and receives binary signals (bits)?
questions.explanation: Physical Layer (Layer 1) is responsible for transmitting raw binary data as electrical, optical, or radio signals.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q40; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 2

questions.question: Which layer converts datagrams into frames?
questions.explanation: Data Link Layer (Layer 2) adds MAC addressing and converts network-layer datagrams into frames for transmission.
hints: This layer performs error detection via CRC
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q47; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 2

questions.question: Which layer defines network addressing and determines the best path?
questions.explanation: Layer 3 (Network) handles logical IP addressing and routing — determining the best path through the internetwork.
hints: IP addresses live at this layer
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q44; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 2

questions.question: Which OSI layer defines data representation, encryption, and format translation?
questions.explanation: Presentation Layer (Layer 6) handles data formatting, translation, and encryption/decryption between application formats.
hints: Think format translation — JPEG, MP3, ASCII
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q39; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 2

bossBattles.name: The OSPF Routing Golem
bossBattles.description: Built from LSAs and SPF trees. Defeat it by mastering link-state routing.

questions.question: What is the default OSPF Hello interval on a broadcast network?
questions.explanation: OSPF Hello interval on broadcast/point-to-point networks defaults to 10 seconds. Dead interval is 4x = 40s.
questions.source_ref: Wendell Odom - CCNA 200-301 OCG Vol 2, Chapter 20: OSPF Concepts

questions.question: Which OSPF LSA type is generated by an ABR to describe routes in another area?
questions.explanation: Type 3 Summary LSAs are generated by ABRs (Area Border Routers) to advertise inter-area routes.
questions.source_ref: Wendell Odom - CCNA 200-301 OCG Vol 2, Chapter 21: OSPF Features

questions.question: What is the OSPF administrative distance in Cisco IOS?
questions.explanation: OSPF has an AD of 110 in Cisco IOS. EIGRP internal=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120.
hints: RIP is 120, OSPF is slightly more trusted
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q10; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 24

bossBattles.name: The ACL Guardian
bossBattles.description: Every packet must justify its existence. One wrong answer and the implicit deny claims you.

questions.question: Which of the following are true about Cisco IOS access lists? (Choose all that apply)
options: Every ACL has an implicit deny all at the end
An ACL should have at least one permit statement
questions.explanation: All statements are true. The implicit deny all at the end is critical — without a permit statement, all traffic is blocked. ACLs process top-down, so order matters.
hints: What happens if no ACE matches?
Once a match is found, processing stops
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q14; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: Standard ACLs should be placed closest to which point?
questions.explanation: Standard ACLs filter only on source IP and lack granularity. Placing them near the destination prevents unintended blocking of valid paths to other destinations.
hints: Extended ACLs go near the source
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q17; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 34: ACLs

questions.question: What happens when 'ip access-group 101 in' is applied to an interface but ACL 101 does not exist?
options: An error message appears and the command is rejected
All traffic in that direction is denied
All traffic in and out is permitted
questions.explanation: When an interface has no ACL applied, it implicitly permits all traffic. The access-group command is accepted even if the ACL doesn't exist yet — and the interface acts as permit all until the ACL is created.
hints: Without an ACL = implicit permit any
The guard analogy: hired but not placed at the gate
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q15; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: Where should an extended ACL be placed for optimal efficiency?
options: As close to the destination as possible
As close to the source as possible
questions.explanation: Extended ACLs should be placed as close to the traffic SOURCE as possible. Because extended ACLs can filter on source, destination, protocol, and port, placing them near the source stops unwanted traffic early and reduces unnecessary load on intermediate links.
hints: Think about where you want to stop unwanted traffic
Standard ACLs go near destination; extended ACLs go near source
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 Ch 3; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 35

questions.question: Named ACLs and numbered ACLs provide identical filtering capabilities on Cisco IOS, but named ACLs allow you to delete individual entries without removing the entire ACL.
questions.explanation: True. Named ACLs (ip access-list standard/extended NAME) offer the same filtering power as numbered ACLs, but their key advantage is the ability to delete specific entries by sequence number. Numbered ACLs traditionally required you to delete and recreate the entire list to remove one entry (though modern IOS also allows sequence-based editing for numbered ACLs).
hints: Named ACLs use: ip access-list standard NAME
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: Which ACL number ranges identify a STANDARD numbered ACL on Cisco IOS?
questions.explanation: Standard numbered ACLs use ranges 1–99 (original range) and 1300–1999 (expanded range). Extended ACLs use 100–199 and 2000–2699. Knowing the ranges lets you instantly identify an ACL's type from its number.
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 Ch 3; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34

questions.question: What is the wildcard mask equivalent of the subnet mask 255.255.252.0?
questions.explanation: A wildcard mask is the bitwise inverse of the subnet mask. 255.255.252.0 inverted gives 0.0.3.255. Calculation: 255-255=0, 255-255=0, 255-252=3, 255-0=255. Wildcard masks are used in ACEs and OSPF network statements.
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: An interface has 'ip access-group 50 out' applied. Traffic flowing OUT of this interface will be filtered. Which statement about inbound traffic on this same interface is correct?
options: Inbound traffic is also filtered by ACL 50
Inbound traffic is permitted without ACL filtering
A separate inbound ACL is automatically created
questions.explanation: An ACL applied with 'out' direction only filters traffic LEAVING the interface. Traffic arriving (inbound) on that same interface is not filtered by ACL 50 — it passes freely unless a separate inbound ACL is configured. Each direction (in/out) must be configured independently.
hints: In and out are independent — only the configured direction is filtered
No ACL on a direction = implicit permit all for that direction
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 Ch 3; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34

questions.question: A router evaluates an ACL with 5 entries against an incoming packet. The packet matches entry 3. What happens next?
options: The router continues checking entries 4 and 5 to find a more specific match
The router applies the action in entry 3 and stops processing the ACL
The router applies all matching entries and uses the most restrictive
The router logs the match and continues to the implicit deny
questions.explanation: ACLs use first-match, top-down processing. Once a packet matches an entry, the corresponding action (permit or deny) is immediately applied and processing STOPS — no further entries are checked. This is why the order of ACEs is critical to correct ACL design.
hints: ACL processing is top-down, first match wins
After a match, IOS does not continue checking
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: Every Cisco IOS ACL ends with an invisible statement that denies all traffic that did not match any explicit entry. This statement is called the implicit ______.
questions.explanation: Every ACL on Cisco IOS ends with an implicit 'deny any' (also called implicit deny all). This means if a packet does not match any explicit permit or deny entry, it is silently dropped. This is why every ACL must contain at least one permit statement, otherwise all traffic is blocked.
hints: It is not visible in 'show access-lists' output
It denies everything that was not explicitly permitted
questions.source_ref: CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide Vol 1 Ch 3; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 34

bossBattles.name: The VLAN Phantom
bossBattles.description: A spectral entity haunting the switching fabric. It materializes misconfigured trunks and phantom VLANs. Answer correctly to banish it from the network.

questions.question: A trunk port is sending frames for VLAN 10 without an 802.1Q tag. What is the most likely reason?
options: VLAN 10 has been pruned from the trunk
VLAN 10 is the native VLAN on this trunk
VLAN 10 does not exist in the VLAN database
The port is configured as an access port
questions.explanation: The native VLAN on an 802.1Q trunk is sent without a VLAN tag. Default native VLAN is VLAN 1, but it can be changed with 'switchport trunk native vlan [id]'.
hints: Think about which VLAN gets special treatment on a trunk

questions.question: SW1 has priority 28672, SW2 has priority 32768 (both VLAN 1). SW1 MAC: AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:01. SW2 MAC: BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:02. Which switch is root and why?
options: SW2 — higher priority wins root election
SW1 — lower bridge ID (lower priority + any MAC) wins
SW2 — higher MAC address breaks the tie
SW1 — lower MAC address wins when priority is equal
questions.explanation: Root bridge = lowest Bridge ID. Bridge ID = priority + MAC. SW1 priority 28672 < SW2 priority 32768, so SW1 wins regardless of MAC. Priority is compared first.
hints: Bridge ID = Priority + MAC (in that order of comparison)

questions.question: RSTP (802.1w) can achieve sub-second convergence and is backward compatible with 802.1D STP.
questions.explanation: RSTP dramatically speeds convergence (often <1 second vs 50 seconds for 802.1D) using its Alternate and Backup port roles and proposal/agreement mechanism. It is backward compatible with 802.1D.
hints: It replaces blocking/listening/learning states with alternate/backup/discarding

questions.question: Which command shows the current DTP negotiation mode and trunk status on a Cisco switch interface?
questions.explanation: 'show interfaces [interface] switchport' shows the admin/oper mode, DTP mode, native VLAN, and trunk encapsulation for a specific port — the most detailed single-interface view.
hints: You need to check a specific interface's L2 settings
The command is at privileged EXEC mode

questions.question: What IOS command is used to verify all EtherChannel bundles and their member port status? (Enter the full command)
questions.explanation: 'show etherchannel summary' is the go-to command for verifying port-channel bundles. It shows the protocol used (LACP/PAgP), port states (P=bundled, I=standalone, S=suspended), and group numbers.
hints: Think about the EtherChannel verification command

questions.question: A port configured as 'switchport mode dynamic desirable' connects to a port configured as 'switchport mode dynamic auto'. What is the resulting operational mode?
options: Both ports remain in access mode
The link becomes a trunk — desirable actively negotiates, auto accepts
The link fails — incompatible DTP modes
Both ports become trunk ports only if VLAN 1 is allowed
questions.explanation: DTP negotiation: dynamic desirable + dynamic auto = trunk. Dynamic desirable actively sends DTP frames; dynamic auto accepts but doesn't initiate. Desirable + desirable = trunk. Auto + auto = access.
hints: 'Desirable' actively tries to form a trunk

questions.question: Which port security violation mode drops frames from violating MACs, increments the violation counter, and sends a syslog message — but does NOT err-disable the port?
questions.explanation: Restrict: drops violating frames + increments counter + sends syslog/SNMP notification. Protect: drops silently (no log). Shutdown (default): err-disables the port. There is no 'err-disable' violation mode.
hints: Three violation modes: shutdown, protect, restrict
Only one mode keeps the port up AND generates logs

bossBattles.name: The Services Hydra
bossBattles.description: A seven-headed beast representing each IP Service. Cut one head and two grow back — unless you know DHCP, NAT, NTP, DNS, SNMP, Syslog, and FHRP cold.

questions.question: A DHCP client sends a DHCPREQUEST broadcast even though it already received a DHCPOFFER. Why does it broadcast instead of unicasting to the server?
options: The client has not yet received an IP address, so it cannot unicast
DHCP always uses broadcast for all messages
Broadcasting notifies other DHCP servers that their offer was declined
Both A and C are correct
questions.explanation: The DHCPREQUEST is broadcast for two reasons: 1) The client doesn't have a confirmed IP yet (can't source a unicast), and 2) Other DHCP servers that sent offers need to know their offer wasn't selected so they can release the reserved address.
hints: The client doesn't have an IP yet during the Request step
Other servers also need to be informed

questions.question: What is the DSCP decimal value for the EF (Expedited Forwarding) Per-Hop Behavior used to mark voice traffic?
questions.explanation: EF (Expedited Forwarding) = DSCP 46 (binary: 101110). It is the standard DSCP marking for VoIP/voice traffic requiring low latency, low jitter, and low packet loss.
hints: EF is used for voice/VoIP traffic
The DSCP value is between 40 and 50

questions.question: A router is configured: 'ip nat inside source list 10 pool NAT-POOL overload'. Access-list 10 permits 192.168.0.0/16. The NAT pool contains 203.0.113.10–203.0.113.20. What type of NAT is this and what is the key effect of 'overload'?
options: Static NAT — one-to-one mapping; overload has no effect
Dynamic PAT — multiple inside hosts share pool IPs, differentiated by port numbers
Dynamic NAT — each inside host gets a unique pool IP; overload means the pool can be reused
Policy NAT — only matching traffic is translated
questions.explanation: 'overload' enables PAT on top of dynamic NAT. Multiple inside hosts can share the same pool IP address, differentiated by unique source port numbers. Without 'overload', each inside host needs its own pool IP.
hints: 'overload' is the keyword that enables PAT

questions.question: HSRP Active router election: Router A has priority 110 and preempt. Router B has priority 120 and NO preempt. Router B is currently active. Router A boots up. What happens?
options: Router A becomes active immediately — it has preempt configured
Router B stays active — it has the higher priority
Router A becomes active — it has the lower priority (wins HSRP election)
Both routers enter active state simultaneously
questions.explanation: HSRP active election: highest priority wins. Router B has priority 120 > Router A's 110. Although Router A has preempt, preempt only matters if the current active has a LOWER priority than the router trying to preempt. B's 120 > A's 110, so B stays active.
hints: Higher HSRP priority wins, not lower
Preempt only works if YOUR priority is HIGHER than the active

questions.question: SNMPv3 provides both authentication (MD5/SHA) and optional privacy (encryption via DES/AES).
questions.explanation: SNMPv3 adds security via three levels: noAuthNoPriv (no auth, no encryption), authNoPriv (auth only), and authPriv (auth + encryption). v1 and v2c use only community strings.
hints: SNMPv3 was designed to fix the security weaknesses of v1 and v2c

questions.question: Which syslog severity level should be configured to capture interface up/down events (severity 5 — Notice) and all more severe messages, while excluding debug?
questions.explanation: Syslog trap level filters messages at that level AND more severe (lower number). 'notifications' = level 5 (Notice). This captures levels 0–5. 'informational' = level 6 (also captures 6 and below). 'warnings' = level 4 (misses level 5 notices).
hints: Syslog level filters: the setting captures that level and all LOWER numbers (more severe)

questions.question: What Cisco IOS command verifies the active NAT translation table showing inside local, inside global, outside local, and outside global addresses?
questions.explanation: 'show ip nat translations' displays the current NAT table. Inside local = pre-NAT private IP. Inside global = post-NAT public IP. Outside local = destination as seen from inside. Outside global = actual destination IP.
hints: The command starts with 'show ip nat'
Think about what you're trying to 'translate'

bossBattles.name: The Automation Oracle
bossBattles.description: An ancient AI forged from Python scripts and YAML playbooks. It speaks only in REST and thinks in JSON. Prove your automation mastery to receive its blessings.

questions.question: A Python script sends 'requests.get(url, headers=headers)' to a REST API and receives HTTP 403. What is the correct interpretation?
options: The resource was not found — check the URL
Authentication failed — check the API token/credentials
The request was authenticated but the user lacks permission for this resource
questions.explanation: 403 Forbidden = authenticated but not authorized. The credentials are valid, but the account lacks the required permission. Compare: 401 = not authenticated at all; 404 = resource not found; 500 = server error.
hints: Authentication (401) vs Authorization (403) — subtle but important
403 means 'I know who you are, but you can't do this'

questions.question: In Python, which function deserializes a JSON string into a Python dictionary? (e.g., used after receiving an API response body as a string)
questions.explanation: json.loads() (load string) converts a JSON string → Python object. json.dumps() converts Python → JSON string. json.load() reads from a file object; json.dump() writes to a file.

questions.question: An Ansible playbook runs against a Cisco IOS device. The task uses 'ios_config' module with 'lines: ["interface Gi0/1", "ip address 10.0.0.1 255.255.255.0"]'. The playbook runs twice. What happens on the second run?
options: The interface IP is configured twice, causing a duplicate entry error
Ansible skips the task because it detects no change is needed (idempotent)
The second run fails because the interface is already configured
Ansible removes the IP and re-configures it to ensure the desired state
questions.explanation: Ansible modules (including ios_config) are designed to be idempotent — if the device already has the desired configuration, Ansible reports 'ok' (no change) and takes no action. This is a core benefit of declarative automation.
hints: Idempotency is a key Ansible property
Running the same playbook twice should be safe

questions.question: What is the role of the vBond orchestrator in Cisco SD-WAN?
options: Forwards all user data traffic between branch sites
Provides the management GUI for policy and monitoring
Distributes OMP routing policies to all vEdge routers
Authenticates new SD-WAN components and facilitates initial connections between controllers and edges
questions.explanation: vBond is the SD-WAN orchestrator — the first contact point for any new SD-WAN component (vEdge, vSmart). It authenticates devices and facilitates their connection to vSmart and vManage. vManage = GUI. vSmart = control plane. vEdge = data plane.
hints: New devices connect to vBond first

questions.question: NETCONF uses XML to encode data and runs over SSH on TCP port 830.
questions.explanation: NETCONF (RFC 6241): Transport = SSH (TCP port 830), Data encoding = XML, Operations = get-config, edit-config, commit, lock, unlock. RESTCONF uses HTTP/HTTPS and supports JSON or XML.
hints: NETCONF and RESTCONF are both used for programmatic device config
One uses SSH, the other uses HTTP

questions.question: A network engineer writes a Python script using Netmiko that connects to a switch, sends 'show ip interface brief', and parses the output with TextFSM. What approach is this an example of?
questions.explanation: Netmiko + TextFSM = SSH screen scraping = imperative approach. You explicitly instruct how to connect, what to send, and parse unstructured CLI output. NETCONF/YANG provides structured data. Ansible is declarative. REST API uses HTTP.
hints: Netmiko sends CLI commands over SSH
Parsing CLI output is the old-school approach vs structured APIs

questions.question: In SDN architecture, which interface connects the SDN controller to business applications and automation systems above it?
options: Northbound Interface (NBI) — e.g., REST API, RESTCONF
questions.explanation: NBI (Northbound Interface) connects the SDN controller upward to applications, orchestration, and automation tools — typically via REST APIs. SBI connects the controller downward to network devices (OpenFlow, NETCONF, gRPC). East/Westbound connects peer controllers.
hints: Think geographically: south = below (to devices), north = above (to apps)
REST APIs are commonly used as the NBI

bossBattles.name: The Breach
bossBattles.description: A zero-day exploit is spreading through the network. Lock it down before it reaches the core.

questions.question: Which command enables SSH version 2 on an IOS router?
questions.explanation: ip ssh version 2 forces the router to use SSHv2 only, which is more secure than v1.

questions.question: Telnet sends data in which format?
questions.explanation: Telnet transmits all data including passwords in cleartext, making it a serious security risk.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'enable password' and 'enable secret'?
options: enable secret uses MD5 hashing; enable password is stored in cleartext by default
enable password uses SHA-256; enable secret uses MD5
enable secret applies only to SSH sessions
questions.explanation: enable secret stores the password as an MD5 hash in the config. enable password stores it in cleartext unless service password-encryption is also configured.

questions.question: Where should an extended ACL be placed for maximum efficiency?
options: As close to the destination as possible
As close to the source as possible
questions.explanation: Extended ACLs should be placed as close to the SOURCE as possible to discard packets early and save bandwidth. Standard ACLs go near the destination.

questions.question: Which attack exploits the default behavior of a native VLAN on a trunk port?
questions.explanation: Double-tagging VLAN hopping sends frames with two 802.1Q headers. The switch strips the outer tag and forwards on the native VLAN, which can reach a target VLAN if it matches the native VLAN.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between IDS and IPS?
options: IDS is hardware; IPS is software
IDS detects and alerts; IPS detects and can block traffic inline
IDS blocks traffic; IPS only monitors
IDS uses signatures; IPS uses anomaly detection only
questions.explanation: IDS (Intrusion Detection System) is passive — it monitors and alerts. IPS (Intrusion Prevention System) is inline and can actively block malicious traffic.

questions.question: Which protocol does TACACS+ use for transport, and what port?
questions.explanation: TACACS+ uses TCP port 49 and encrypts the entire payload. RADIUS uses UDP 1812/1813 and only encrypts the password.

questions.question: What happens when AAA is configured with 'aaa authentication login default local' and the TACACS+ server is unreachable?
options: All logins are denied (fail-closed by default)
The router falls back to the local user database
questions.explanation: With 'aaa authentication login default local', the router uses the local database. If 'group tacacs+ local' is specified, it tries the server first then falls back to local. Without 'local', server failure = fail-closed.

questions.question: In Zone-Based Firewall (ZBF), what is the default action for traffic between interfaces in different security zones?
options: Drop all traffic — no policy = deny
questions.explanation: ZBF uses a default-deny model between zones. Traffic between zones is dropped unless an explicit zone-pair with a service-policy permits or inspects it.

questions.question: Which component of IPsec provides data integrity verification?
options: HMAC (e.g., HMAC-SHA-256) in AH or ESP
questions.explanation: HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code) provides integrity. AH uses HMAC for integrity without encryption. ESP can use both HMAC for integrity AND AES for confidentiality.

questions.question: BPDU Guard places a port in err-disabled state when it receives a BPDU. Which scenario would trigger this?
options: An uplink trunk port receiving BPDUs from another switch
A PortFast-enabled access port receiving a BPDU from a rogue switch
A root port receiving an inferior BPDU
A designated port forwarding BPDUs to access devices
questions.explanation: BPDU Guard is typically enabled on PortFast ports (access ports). If an unauthorized switch is connected and sends BPDUs, BPDU Guard shuts the port (err-disable), preventing topology manipulation.

questions.question: What distinguishes a stateful firewall from a packet-filtering ACL?
options: Stateful firewalls use hardware; ACLs use software
Stateful firewalls track connection state (SYN/ESTABLISHED) and allow return traffic automatically
ACLs track session state; stateful firewalls only check headers
Stateful firewalls only work with UDP; ACLs handle TCP
questions.explanation: Stateful firewalls maintain a connection state table. Return traffic for established sessions is automatically permitted. ACLs are stateless — you must explicitly permit return traffic with rules like 'established' or separate rules.

questions.question: Which DHCP snooping feature prevents a rogue DHCP server on an access port?
options: Marking the access port as a trusted port
Marking the access port as untrusted — only trusted ports forward DHCP offers
Enabling dynamic ARP inspection on the port
Applying an inbound ACL blocking UDP port 67
questions.explanation: DHCP snooping marks uplinks/legitimate server ports as trusted. Untrusted ports (access ports) drop DHCP OFFER and ACK messages, preventing rogue DHCP servers.

questions.question: The 'service password-encryption' command uses Type 7 (Vigenere cipher) encryption, which is considered cryptographically weak.
questions.explanation: Type 7 encryption is easily reversed with freely available tools. It only prevents shoulder-surfing from a printed config. Use 'enable secret' (Type 5 MD5) or Type 8/9 for real security.

questions.question: An attacker sends thousands of DHCP DISCOVER messages with spoofed source MACs. What is this attack called and what does it cause?
options: ARP Poisoning — corrupts the ARP cache
DHCP Starvation — exhausts the DHCP pool so legitimate hosts cannot get an IP
MAC Flooding — overflows the CAM table
questions.explanation: DHCP Starvation exhausts the IP address pool. The attacker then often sets up a rogue DHCP server to serve malicious DNS/gateway info. Mitigated with DHCP Snooping rate limiting on untrusted ports.

bossBattles.name: The Migration
bossBattles.description: The CTO just mandated a full cloud migration. Prove you know IaaS from SaaS — and survive the cutover.

questions.question: A company moves its email system to Microsoft 365. Which cloud service model is this?
questions.explanation: SaaS (Software as a Service) — the provider manages everything, the customer only consumes the application. Microsoft 365, Gmail, and Salesforce are classic SaaS examples.

questions.question: Which cloud service model gives you the most control over the operating system and runtime?
questions.explanation: IaaS (Infrastructure as a Service) provides virtual machines where you install and manage the OS, middleware, and applications. PaaS abstracts the OS away; SaaS abstracts everything.

questions.question: What is a cloud Availability Zone (AZ)?
options: A geographic region containing multiple cloud data centres
An isolated data centre facility within a cloud region
A virtual network segment in the cloud
A cloud provider's point of presence for CDN
questions.explanation: An Availability Zone is an isolated data centre (or group of DCs) within a Region with independent power, cooling, and networking. Deploying across AZs provides fault tolerance against AZ-level failure.

questions.question: What is the cloud equivalent of a physical network or VLAN environment?
questions.explanation: A VPC (Virtual Private Cloud) is a logically isolated virtual network you define in the cloud — analogous to your physical on-prem network or VLAN infrastructure.

questions.question: A Security Group in AWS differs from a Network ACL in which key way?
options: Security Groups are stateless; Network ACLs are stateful
Security Groups are stateful (return traffic auto-permitted); Network ACLs are stateless
Security Groups apply at the subnet level; NACLs apply to instances
questions.explanation: Security Groups are stateful — like a firewall, return traffic for established connections is automatically permitted. NACLs are stateless — like an ACL, you must explicitly allow both inbound and outbound directions.

questions.question: Which cloud component allows instances in a private subnet to initiate outbound internet traffic without being directly reachable from the internet?
questions.explanation: A NAT Gateway allows private subnet instances to reach the internet (e.g., for software updates) while blocking all unsolicited inbound connections — similar to a NAT router in an on-prem network.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of a hybrid cloud deployment model?
options: Eliminates the need for on-premises infrastructure entirely
Allows sensitive workloads to remain on-prem while bursting to public cloud for peak demand
Reduces latency by placing all workloads in a single location
Provides free unlimited cloud resources during peak periods
questions.explanation: Hybrid cloud combines private (on-prem or hosted) and public cloud. Organisations keep sensitive/regulated data on-prem for compliance while using public cloud for scalability and less sensitive workloads.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes SD-WAN's advantage over traditional WAN?
options: SD-WAN eliminates the need for encryption on WAN links
SD-WAN uses a centralised controller to manage multiple WAN transports with application-aware routing and automatic failover
SD-WAN replaces all IP routing with MPLS label switching
SD-WAN requires dedicated hardware at every branch
questions.explanation: SD-WAN abstracts the WAN transport (MPLS, broadband, LTE) behind a software control plane. It provides application-aware routing (voice over MPLS, bulk over broadband), automatic failover, and centralised policy management.

questions.question: SASE (Secure Access Service Edge) converges which two domains?
options: Networking (SD-WAN) and security (FWaaS, CASB, ZTNA, SWG) delivered from the cloud
questions.explanation: SASE converges SD-WAN networking with cloud-delivered security services: FWaaS (Firewall-as-a-Service), CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker), ZTNA (Zero Trust Network Access), and SWG (Secure Web Gateway).

questions.question: In Infrastructure as Code (IaC), which tool is considered agentless and uses SSH/WinRM to configure managed nodes?
questions.explanation: Ansible is agentless — it connects to managed nodes via SSH (Linux) or WinRM (Windows) with no daemon required. Puppet and Chef require agents installed on managed nodes. This simplifies management but can be slower at scale.

questions.question: Terraform differs from Ansible in that Terraform primarily focuses on:
options: Infrastructure provisioning (creating/destroying cloud resources) using a declarative model
Real-time monitoring and alerting of cloud infrastructure
questions.explanation: Terraform (HashiCorp) is an Infrastructure as Code tool focused on provisioning infrastructure (VMs, networks, DNS, etc.) in a declarative, idempotent way. Ansible is better suited for configuration management after infrastructure exists.

questions.question: In a public cloud model, the customer is responsible for the physical security of the data centre hardware.
questions.explanation: In any cloud model, the provider is responsible for physical security of the data centre. The Shared Responsibility Model defines what the customer vs provider manages — physical facilities are always the provider's responsibility.

questions.question: What is a CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker)?
options: A VPN concentrator placed in front of cloud applications
A security enforcement point between users and cloud services that provides visibility, compliance, and threat protection for SaaS
A load balancer for distributing traffic across cloud regions
A certificate authority for issuing cloud TLS certificates
questions.explanation: A CASB sits between users and cloud applications (SaaS/IaaS) enforcing security policies: DLP, access control, anomaly detection, compliance. It provides visibility into shadow IT (unauthorised SaaS usage).

questions.question: Zero Trust Architecture differs from traditional perimeter security in that it:
options: Trusts all traffic inside the corporate network
Requires no authentication for internal users
Assumes no implicit trust based on network location — every access request is verified regardless of source
Eliminates the need for firewalls entirely
questions.explanation: Zero Trust: 'Never trust, always verify.' No device or user gets implicit trust because they are on the internal network. Every request requires authentication, authorisation, and is continuously validated. Protects against lateral movement after breach.

questions.question: Which cloud deployment model is used exclusively by organisations with a shared regulatory or mission requirement, such as government agencies?
questions.explanation: Community Cloud is shared infrastructure managed for a specific group with common concerns (compliance, security, jurisdiction). Examples: FedRAMP (US government), healthcare sector clouds, financial industry shared platforms.

storyBeats.dialogue: You the new junior? Good. Put your bag down. Don't touch the console on the left — that's production. The one on the right is yours if you earn it.

choices.text: Understood. What am I walking into?

storyBeats.dialogue: ZeroNet runs infrastructure for eleven clients. Any of them goes down, we know in under three minutes. This NOC exists so that doesn't happen. That's the entire job description.

choices.text: Got it. What do I start on?

storyBeats.dialogue: Three this quarter. One was a misconfigured trunk. One was a routing loop. One was a vendor update nobody documented. All preventable. All traced back to someone who skipped the fundamentals.
Before you touch a live config, I need to know your foundations hold. Take the Week 1 assessment. It's not long. I'll know if you guessed your way through it.

choices.text: One question first — what should I prioritize?

storyBeats.dialogue: OSI and TCP/IP. Not because the exam asks — because every troubleshooting conversation happens in that framework. Know the layers. Know what lives at each one.

choices.text: Physical to application. I know it.

storyBeats.dialogue: Two protocols live at Layer 4. TCP is connection-oriented — it opens with a three-way handshake: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK. It guarantees delivery through acknowledgements and retransmits lost segments. Every HTTP, SSH, and FTP session uses it. UDP skips all that — no handshake, no retransmission. Lower overhead, higher speed. DNS lookups, DHCP, VoIP, and video streaming use UDP because latency matters more than retrying a lost packet. The exam tests which protocol fits which application. Know them both.

concept_visual.title: TCP 3-Way Handshake & Teardown
concept_visual.explanation: Step through the SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK handshake that establishes every TCP connection. Try the Handshake Challenge to test yourself — order the flags correctly to earn XP.

storyBeats.dialogue: Port numbers identify services. Well-known ports: SSH=22, Telnet=23, SMTP=25, DNS=53, HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, FTP=20/21, DHCP=67/68, RDP=3389, SNMP=161/162. TCP carries SSH, HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, SMTP. UDP carries DNS, DHCP, SNMP, TFTP, VoIP. Some services use both — DNS uses UDP for queries under 512 bytes, TCP for zone transfers. When the exam gives you a scenario and asks which port or protocol, map it to the service. Memorise that list. It will appear.

concept_visual.title: Well-Known Port Numbers
concept_visual.explanation: Filter and search every CCNA-relevant port. Know which are TCP vs UDP, which protocol they serve, and which direction traffic flows — examiners love port-to-protocol matching.

storyBeats.dialogue: A switch builds a MAC address table. When a frame arrives, the switch records the source MAC and the ingress port — that’s learning. If the destination MAC isn’t in the table, the switch floods the frame out every port except the source — unknown unicast flooding. When the destination replies, its MAC gets learned and future frames are forwarded only to that port. Entries age out after 300 seconds by default. Broadcast frames flood always — every port, every time. This is all Layer 2. No IP involved.

concept_visual.title: ARP Resolution & Cache
concept_visual.explanation: Before a switch can forward a frame, a host must resolve the destination MAC via ARP. Step through the broadcast request, unicast reply, and the resulting ARP cache entry.

storyBeats.dialogue: IPv4 has 4.3 billion addresses — exhausted. IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses written as eight groups of four hex digits, separated by colons. Types: Global Unicast (2000::/3) — routable on the internet, like a public IPv4. Link-local (FE80::/10) — auto-configured on every IPv6 interface, never forwarded beyond the local link. Unique Local (FC00::/7) — private, like RFC 1918. SLAAC lets a host self-configure using the router prefix plus an EUI-64 derived from its MAC address: the MAC is split in half, FF:FE inserted in the middle. No DHCP server required.

IPv4 exhaustion is why we need NAT. Three private ranges defined in RFC 1918 that are never routed on the internet: 10.0.0.0/8 (Class A private — 16 million addresses), 172.16.0.0/12 (Class B private — covers 172.16.0.0 through 172.31.255.255), 192.168.0.0/16 (Class C private — 65,536 addresses). Every enterprise uses these internally. A router at the edge runs NAT to translate private IPs to a public routable address. The exam will give you an IP and ask if it’s private or public. Know the three ranges cold.

Three-tier: Access layer connects end devices, Distribution aggregates and enforces policy, Core provides high-speed backbone. Two-tier collapses distribution into core — smaller environments. Spine-leaf: every leaf switch connects to every spine, no leaf-to-leaf links. Flat, predictable latency, built for data centres. SOHO: one device handles routing, switching, WiFi, and NAT for a home office. Match architecture to scale: SOHO for home, two-tier for mid-size, three-tier for enterprise, spine-leaf for data centre. The exam gives you a scenario and expects you to identify the right model.

Copper UTP: Cat5e supports 1Gbps to 100 metres. Cat6 supports 10Gbps to 55 metres (1Gbps to 100m). Cat6a supports 10Gbps to 100 metres. Straight-through cables connect unlike devices (PC to switch, router to switch). Crossover cables connect like devices (switch to switch) — but auto-MDIX on modern equipment handles this automatically. Fibre: single-mode (yellow jacket, SMF) uses laser light for multi-kilometre runs. Multimode (orange or aqua jacket, MMF) uses LED or VCSEL for distances up to a few hundred metres. SFP or SFP+ modules plug into switch ports for fibre. PoE standards: 802.3af = 15.4 W, 802.3at = 30 W, 802.3bt = 90 W.

Server virtualisation lets one physical machine run multiple VMs. Each VM has its own OS and virtual NIC (vNIC). A hypervisor manages resource allocation. Type 1 (bare-metal) runs directly on hardware — VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, KVM. Type 2 (hosted) runs on top of an existing OS — VirtualBox. Containers are lighter: they share the host OS kernel, package only the application and its dependencies — Docker. Virtual switches connect VMs to each other and to physical NICs. Network engineers encounter vNICs and virtual switches constantly in modern data centres. Exam: Type 1 vs Type 2 distinction, and VM versus container.
Marcus just called. User on Floor 5, 'internet is down.' Before I send anyone up there — what layer do you start troubleshooting from?

choices.text: Layer 1. Check the physical connection first.
Layer 3. Probably a routing or IP issue.
Layer 7. Could be an application problem.

concept_visual.title: OSI Model — Troubleshooting Layers
concept_visual.explanation: Use the OSI model as a systematic framework. Start at Layer 1 (Physical) and work up, or top-down from Layer 7. Each layer adds headers that help isolate the fault.

storyBeats.dialogue: Correct. Check the link light before you open a ticket. Walking up there takes two minutes. If it's physical, you just saved everyone an hour of chasing ghosts in the config.

choices.text: Start at Layer 1, work up. Got it.

storyBeats.dialogue: Could be. But if their cable is unplugged, you've wasted ten minutes pulling routing tables. Physical takes thirty seconds to rule out. Bottom of the stack, always.
You'd spend twenty minutes on the application before noticing the cable is half out of the wall. Start at Layer 1 and work up. The physical layer doesn't lie.
Hey — new person. Maya said you passed the assessment. There's a switch in Lab 101 that needs base config before it goes to the floor. No hostname, no passwords, nothing. She said it's yours.

choices.text: Rollover cable, console port. I've got it.

storyBeats.dialogue: Maya left a note: hostname, enable secret, interface IPs, no shutdown. In that order. She also wrote 'verify before you call it done' and underlined it twice. I don't know what that means. She does.

choices.text: Show running-config before and after. Standard.

storyBeats.dialogue: Three modes that matter: User EXEC, Privileged EXEC, and Global Config. You can only break things from Global Config — which is why it requires 'configure terminal' to get there.

choices.text: Privilege mode is 'enable'. Config is one level deeper.

storyBeats.dialogue: hostname> is User EXEC — limited, read-mostly. hostname# is Privileged EXEC — you can see everything. hostname(config)# is Global Config — you can change everything. The prompt tells you where you are.

choices.text: The prompt is the mode indicator. Got it.

storyBeats.dialogue: The switch is waiting. Hostname, enable secret, interface configs. Interfaces are administratively down by default — don't forget 'no shutdown'. Verify when you're done. Not before.
Lab's done. One more thing before I call day one finished. The OSI Titan — a full gauntlet across every layer. It's how I know if someone's going to be useful in this NOC or a liability.

choices.text: How many people have passed it?

storyBeats.dialogue: Everyone still here. The ones who didn't — they left voluntarily, mostly. Knowing what you don't know is also a skill. Go. I'll have eyes on the alert board.
Titan's down. That's day one. You didn't break anything and you know your layers. Tomorrow is VLANs — Ravi runs the switch stack training. Be here at eight. He starts without you.

So Maya sent you to me. Good. That means Week 1 stuck. Welcome to the data center. This row right here is the access layer — 48 ports per switch, six switches, three VLANs that should be five. Somebody made a mess and I've been living in it for three weeks.

Three Fridays ago, Floor 4 lost connectivity for 90 minutes. Not routing. Not the firewall. A broadcast storm. Someone plugged a switch into two ports on the same VLAN without STP enabled. Loop formed. Frames multiplied until the CPU on the core switch hit 100% and stopped forwarding anything. Entire floor, dark.

choices.text: How do you detect it while it's happening?

storyBeats.dialogue: STP. Spanning Tree Protocol. It's been running silently in every properly configured network since 1985. It maps the physical topology, elects a root bridge, and blocks redundant paths so loops can't form. The problem is it only works if it's turned on and configured correctly. That switch someone plugged in? PortFast enabled, BPDU Guard disabled. A disaster waiting to happen.

choices.text: Got it. What are VLANs for?

concept_visual.title: STP Port State Timeline
concept_visual.explanation: See exactly how long STP takes in each state — click a segment to understand Blocking, Listening, Learning, and Forwarding. Compare Classic 802.1D (50s) vs RSTP (<1s) vs PortFast.

storyBeats.dialogue: You check interface counters. 'show interfaces' on the core switch — if input errors and runts are spiking, something's wrong at Layer 2. CPU utilization climbing with no traffic increase is another red flag. Then you start unplugging suspects. It's not elegant, but it works. STP is how you prevent it in the first place.

Think of a switch as a highway with 48 lanes. By default, every car — every frame — shares all lanes. VLANs add dividers. VLAN 10 is Sales. VLAN 20 is Engineering. VLAN 30 is Management. Traffic in VLAN 10 cannot reach VLAN 20 at Layer 2. Ever. They're logically separate networks sharing the same physical wire.

choices.text: So how does traffic get between VLANs?

concept_visual.title: VLAN Segmentation
concept_visual.explanation: See how VLANs divide a single physical switch into isolated broadcast domains. Each VLAN is an independent segment — traffic cannot cross without a Layer 3 device.

storyBeats.dialogue: Layer 3. A router or a Layer 3 switch with SVIs — Switched Virtual Interfaces. You assign an IP to each VLAN interface and the router handles routing between them. Without that, VLAN 10 and VLAN 20 are completely isolated. Which is usually what you want. Finance shouldn't be browsing Engineering's file server.

A trunk port carries traffic for multiple VLANs on one cable. It uses 802.1Q — a 4-byte tag inserted into the Ethernet frame that identifies the VLAN. Switch-to-switch links are trunks. Switch-to-router links can be trunks. Switch-to-PC — that's an access port. One VLAN, no tags. The PC never even knows VLANs exist.

choices.text: Which port type goes to an end-user workstation?

storyBeats.dialogue: To route between VLANs you need Layer 3. Router-on-a-stick: one physical router interface trunked to the switch, multiple sub-interfaces. Each gets 'encapsulation dot1q <vlan-id>' and an IP address — that IP becomes the default gateway for that VLAN. Fa0/0.10 for VLAN 10, Fa0/0.20 for VLAN 20. The physical interface itself has no IP. Layer 3 switch alternative: create interface vlan 10, assign an IP, no shutdown — that’s an SVI. Faster in hardware, preferred in production. The exam tests both configurations. Know the CLI for each.

choices.text: How does STP elect the root bridge?

storyBeats.dialogue: Root bridge election: lowest Bridge ID wins. Bridge ID = Priority (default 32768 plus VLAN ID) plus MAC address. Adjust priority in multiples of 4096. Non-root bridges select a Root Port — lowest-cost path to root. STP cost: 1Gbps=4, 100Mbps=19, 10Mbps=100. Each segment elects a Designated Port; all others become Alternate and enter Blocking. Port states: Blocking, Listening, Learning, Forwarding. RSTP converges in under a second via Proposal/Agreement. PortFast skips listening and learning on access ports — enable only on ports facing end devices. BPDU Guard shuts a PortFast port the moment it receives a BPDU — protects topology from rogue switches.

concept_visual.title: Spanning Tree Protocol
concept_visual.explanation: Step through STP root bridge election, port role assignment, and state transitions. Adjust priorities and see which paths get blocked to prevent Layer 2 loops.

storyBeats.dialogue: EtherChannel bundles up to eight physical links into one logical link. STP sees one path so nothing gets blocked. Protocols: LACP (IEEE 802.3ad) is the open standard — use this. PAgP is Cisco proprietary. All member ports must match: same speed, duplex, VLAN config, and trunk settings. A mismatch suspends the bundle. Load balances using a hash of source and destination MAC or IP depending on configuration. Verify with 'show etherchannel summary'. Exam: which protocol is open standard? LACP. What happens on a port mismatch? Ports are suspended.

Autonomous APs: self-contained, each configured individually. Fine for one or two APs, unmanageable at scale. Lightweight APs: zero configuration on the AP. They join a Wireless LAN Controller via CAPWAP — UDP ports 5246 and 5247. The WLC pushes all configuration, manages RF channels, security, and client roaming. Non-overlapping 2.4GHz channels: 1, 6, 11. 5GHz has many more non-overlapping channels and higher throughput. Security: WPA2-Personal uses a pre-shared key. WPA2-Enterprise uses 802.1X with a RADIUS server for per-user authentication. WPA3 replaces PSK with SAE — stronger, resistant to offline dictionary attacks.
You've got a new workstation going into VLAN 30 — the management VLAN. The port needs to be configured. What mode do you set it to?

choices.text: Trunk mode — the PC might need multiple VLANs.
Dynamic auto — let the switch negotiate.

storyBeats.dialogue: Exactly. Access port, VLAN 30. The PC doesn't know about VLANs — it just sends Ethernet frames. The switch handles the tagging internally. End devices get access ports. Switches get trunk ports. If you trunk to a regular PC, the port stays up but the PC drops the 802.1Q-tagged frames and nothing works.

choices.text: Makes sense. What should I study before the quiz?

storyBeats.dialogue: Not quite. Standard workstations don't process 802.1Q tags — they'll drop the frames. Trunk ports are for switch-to-switch and switch-to-router links. For an end device in one VLAN, you use access mode. The switch handles tagging internally. Access port, VLAN 30, done.

DTP negotiation with a workstation — never. The PC won't respond to DTP, the port ends up in access mode anyway, but you've left the negotiation open. Attackers exploit DTP to coax switches into trunking with rogue devices. Always set explicitly: 'switchport mode access' or 'switchport mode trunk.' No ambiguity in production.

Before the quiz — three things that trip everyone up: native VLAN mismatches on trunks cause STP topology issues and traffic bleed between VLANs. Forgetting 'no shutdown' on an SVI leaves inter-VLAN routing dead. And trunk ports default to allowing all 4094 VLANs, which you should always restrict. Know those three cold.

choices.text: Go to The Grind and take the quiz.

storyBeats.dialogue: Ravi, ticket just came in. Floor 3 users are seeing Floor 5 broadcast traffic. Sales and Engineering are bleeding into each other. I don't know how — they're supposed to be on different VLANs.

choices.text: Native VLAN mismatch on a trunk?
Sounds like a port got switched to trunk mode by mistake.

storyBeats.dialogue: That's my first guess too. If the native VLAN is VLAN 1 on one end of the trunk and VLAN 30 on the other, untagged frames from VLAN 30 arrive on VLAN 1. Two VLANs start mixing. CDP will warn you — 'Native VLAN mismatch detected on trunk.' The lab is built around exactly this scenario.

Here's the lab checklist. Create VLANs 10, 20, 30 — name them Sales, Engineering, Management. Set Fa0/1 as a trunk, allowed VLANs 10, 20, 30. Set Fa0/2 as access for VLAN 10. Verify with 'show vlan brief' and 'show interfaces trunk.' Maya's rule applies here too: verify before you call it done.

choices.text: Do I set the native VLAN on the trunk?

storyBeats.dialogue: Yes, and it matters. Best practice: set native VLAN to something unused — VLAN 999. VLAN 1 is the default native and also the default management VLAN, which is a security risk. Separate them. Use 'switchport trunk native vlan 999' after creating the VLAN, and make sure both ends of the trunk match. Mismatch here is how broadcast storms start.

choices.text: VLAN 999 for native. Starting the lab.

storyBeats.dialogue: Lab's done. VLANs are clean, trunks are right. But there's something in this data center that's been bothering me for months. We call it the VLAN Phantom. Traffic appearing on VLANs where it shouldn't exist. Ports in states that don't match the config. Like the network has a ghost.

choices.text: Or a rogue device on the network.

storyBeats.dialogue: Both. The Phantom is a gauntlet — misconfigured trunks, incorrect VLAN assignments, STP topology questions, port modes, native VLANs. If you know your Layer 2 cold, it's nothing. If you've got any gaps, it will find them. Maya said Week 1 was to see if you'd survive. This one's to see if you can actually think.

choices.text: I've been in the labs. I'm ready.

storyBeats.dialogue: Ravi says you exorcised the Phantom. That's Week 2 done — Layer 2 is clean territory for you now. Week 3 is routing. Priya from the IP connectivity team runs it. She doesn't do hand-holding and she opens with OSPF on day one. Get some sleep.
Welcome to the ISP Handoff Room. Every packet leaving this building crosses this room. Right now there’s a routing loop to 10.10.20.0 active. We need to kill it before the SLA timer hits zero.

Confession: at my last job I put a wrong network statement in OSPF and took down an ISP link for 20 minutes. Everyone was paged. CEO called. I’ve never made that mistake twice. Today, someone else made it.

OSPF is a link-state protocol. Every router builds a complete map of the network via LSAs, then runs Dijkstra’s SPF algorithm to find the shortest path. No hop-count limit. Fast convergence. It’s why we use it.

choices.text: How does it differ from RIP?

concept_visual.title: OSPF Link-State Routing
concept_visual.explanation: Walk through OSPF neighbor adjacency, LSA flooding, and the SPF algorithm. See how every router builds the same complete topology map before computing best paths.

storyBeats.dialogue: RIP is distance-vector: routers only share their routing tables with neighbours. Max 15 hops — anything beyond is unreachable. Slow to converge. Fine for tiny labs. Never for production. OSPF wins every time.

choices.text: What are Hello and Dead timers?

storyBeats.dialogue: OSPF neighbours exchange Hello packets to prove they’re alive. Hello=10s on point-to-point. Dead=40s (4x Hello). If no Hello arrives within Dead interval, neighbour is declared down and SPF reruns. Mismatch = no adjacency.

On multiaccess segments like Ethernet, every router forming adjacency with every other is noisy. OSPF elects a DR and BDR. All others form adjacency only with them. Highest router-ID or priority wins. Priority 0 = never DR.
AD is the router’s trust ranking. Lower = more trusted. Static route: 1. OSPF: 110. RIP: 120. EIGRP internal: 90. If two protocols advertise the same prefix, the lower AD wins the RIB entry.

concept_visual.title: Routing Protocol AD & Route Selection
concept_visual.explanation: Compare Administrative Distance values across all routing sources. When the same prefix arrives from two protocols the lower AD wins — drag-and-drop the AD values to test yourself.

storyBeats.dialogue: Quick check. What is the administrative distance of OSPF?
Correct. OSPF AD is 110. 90 is EIGRP. 120 is RIP. Lower AD wins when two sources advertise the same prefix. This is foundational — you’ll see it on the exam and in production.
90 is EIGRP, not OSPF. Easy to mix these up. OSPF=110, EIGRP internal=90, RIP=120. Memorise that list — the exam loves to swap them. OSPF is 110.

choices.text: Got it. What about wildcard masks?

storyBeats.dialogue: 120 is RIP, not OSPF. They’re neighbours on the list but different protocols. OSPF=110. RIP=120. OSPF is more trusted because its AD is lower. Fix that in your notes now.

Wildcard masks are inverse subnet masks. 0=must match, 1=don’t care. /24 subnet mask 255.255.255.0 becomes wildcard 0.0.0.255. OSPF network statements use them. Get this wrong and you advertise the wrong prefix — ask me how I know.

IPv6 routing requires one global command: 'ipv6 unicast-routing'. Without it the router treats IPv6 as host-only. Assign an address to an interface: 'ipv6 address 2001:DB8:1::1/64'. Static IPv6 route: 'ipv6 route 2001:DB8:2::/64 2001:DB8:1::2'. Default route: 'ipv6 route ::/0 <next-hop>'. Verify with 'show ipv6 route' and 'show ipv6 interface brief'. OSPFv3 is the IPv6 version of OSPF — it’s configured per-interface with 'ipv6 ospf <process> area <area>' rather than via network statements. The exam tests static IPv6 routes and recognising whether ipv6 unicast-routing is missing when IPv6 traffic won’t route.

All OSPF areas must connect to area 0, the backbone. Non-backbone areas exchange LSAs through area 0. If you partition area 0, routing breaks in ways that are miserable to debug at 2am. Always connect to backbone.

choices.text: Study tips before I hit the quiz?

storyBeats.dialogue: If your default gateway fails, every host on that segment loses connectivity even if a backup router exists. First Hop Redundancy Protocols fix this. HSRP (Cisco) and VRRP (open standard) share a virtual IP and virtual MAC. The active router owns the virtual IP; the standby monitors via Hello messages. If the active fails, the standby takes over in seconds. Hosts configure the virtual IP as their gateway — they never know which physical router is active. Election: highest priority wins, default 100; ties broken by highest IP address. Preemption must be enabled for the higher-priority router to take over after recovery. GLBP load-balances across multiple routers simultaneously.

concept_visual.title: HSRP / VRRP / GLBP — First-Hop Redundancy
concept_visual.explanation: See how HSRP pairs an Active and Standby router behind a single virtual IP. Hosts never know a failover happened — their default gateway address never changes.

storyBeats.dialogue: Memorise AD values: Static=1, OSPF=110, RIP=120. Sketch the SPF tree on paper at least once. Know that Hello/Dead mismatch = no neighbour. And never — ever — copy-paste a network statement without double-checking the wildcard.
Ticket #4471: 'Internet is down for the whole 4th floor.' Turns out the OSPF adjacency to the ISP router dropped. Maya says it’s your lab now. Three scenarios, one root cause. Good luck.
Three labs: configure multi-area OSPF with correct wildcard masks, set up RIPv2 with passive interfaces, and add static routes with correct AD. Fix the loop before you close the ticket.

choices.text: Which lab should I start with?

storyBeats.dialogue: Start with the RIPv2 easy lab to build muscle memory. Then static routes. Save the OSPF hard lab for last — it requires everything. And check your wildcard masks twice. I’m not bailing you out.

Checklist: verify neighbour adjacency with 'show ip ospf neighbor', check routing table with 'show ip route', confirm Hello/Dead timers match on both ends, and validate area assignments. 'debug ip ospf events' if stuck.

The loop is gone. Adjacency is up. But the monitoring system just flagged something in the routing table — a ghost route that shouldn’t exist. Something is generating LSAs it shouldn’t be. The OSPF Golem is active.

A misconfigured router flooding the network with corrupted LSAs. It’s overwhelmed the SPF process on two ABRs. Take it down: clear the bad LSAs, fix the network statements, restore convergence. This is a timed challenge.

Golem neutralised. ISP link green. You handled that cleaner than I expected. Routing is solid now — but the services layer above it is held together with duct tape. Ravi’s in the server room. Go find him.

Floor 3 sales office is down. Every machine got a 169.254 address. I know what happened — it’s my config. Scope exhausted and the relay broke at the same time. I designed this. I have to fix it. Let’s go.

Two failures hit at once: the DHCP scope for the sales VLAN ran out of leases, and the ip helper-address on the access switch got wiped during a maintenance window. No relay, no DHCP, APIPA kicks in. 169.254.x.x everywhere.

DHCP is four messages: Discover (broadcast from client), Offer (server proposes IP), Request (client asks for it), Acknowledge (server confirms). DORA. If any step fails, no address. If relay is missing, Discover never reaches the server.

concept_visual.title: DHCP DORA Flow
concept_visual.explanation: Step through all four DHCP messages: Discover, Offer, Request, and Acknowledge. See the exact source/destination IPs and why each is broadcast or unicast.

storyBeats.dialogue: DHCP server config on IOS. First, exclude the gateway and any static-assigned addresses so they’re never handed out: 'ip dhcp excluded-address 10.10.1.1 10.10.1.10'. Then create the pool: 'ip dhcp pool SALES' — then 'network 10.10.1.0 255.255.255.0', 'default-router 10.10.1.1', 'dns-server 8.8.8.8', 'lease 1' (one day). Verify with 'show ip dhcp binding' to see which client got which IP, 'show ip dhcp pool' to check scope utilisation, and 'show ip dhcp conflict' to find address conflicts. If a host gets 169.254.x.x, check the scope, the relay, and the server in that order.

APIPA: Automatic Private IP Addressing. When DHCP fails, Windows and Mac self-assign a 169.254.0.0/16 address. It means: DHCP is broken. Full stop. 169.254 on a host is your canary. Always check the relay and scope first.

DHCP broadcasts don’t cross routers. ip helper-address tells the router to forward the broadcast as a unicast to the DHCP server’s IP. It’s configured on the router interface facing the client. One line. If it’s wrong, sales is down.

concept_visual.title: DHCP Relay Agent (ip helper-address)
concept_visual.explanation: Switch to Relay mode and see how ip helper-address converts client broadcasts into unicasts, inserts the giaddr field, and routes the exchange to a remote DHCP server.

storyBeats.dialogue: DNS translates hostnames to IP addresses. Without it every application needs hardcoded IPs. Process: client asks its resolver (configured via DHCP as 'dns-server'). Resolver checks cache; if not found, queries root servers, then TLD server, then authoritative server, which returns the record. Record types: A for IPv4, AAAA for IPv6, MX for mail, CNAME for aliases. DNS uses UDP port 53 for standard queries under 512 bytes. TCP port 53 for larger responses and zone transfers. On IOS: 'ip name-server 8.8.8.8' sets the DNS server; 'ip domain-name corp.local' appends the domain suffix for hostname resolution.

concept_visual.title: DNS — Recursive vs Iterative Resolution
concept_visual.explanation: Walk through the full DNS lookup chain from client to Root NS to TLD to authoritative. Switch between recursive (resolver does the work) and iterative (client follows referrals) modes.

storyBeats.dialogue: SNMP lets a Network Management System monitor and control devices. Components: Manager (NMS), Agent (software on device), MIB (database of variables). OIDs are dotted-notation paths to specific values. SNMPv2c uses community strings — plain text, no encryption. 'public' for read-only, 'private' for read-write. Never leave these defaults in production. SNMPv3 adds authentication (MD5 or SHA) and encryption (DES or AES) — always use v3. Traps: unsolicited alerts sent from agent to manager. Informs: like traps but the manager sends an acknowledgement. The exam tests: v2c vs v3 security differences, and trap vs inform.

concept_visual.title: SNMP Manager / Agent / MIB
concept_visual.explanation: Explore how an NMS polls device agents using OID paths in the MIB, and how Traps and Informs push unsolicited alerts upstream. Compare SNMPv1 vs v2c vs v3 security models.

storyBeats.dialogue: A host shows IP 169.254.40.12 with mask 255.255.0.0. What does this tell you?

choices.text: DHCP worked, it’s a valid address
DHCP failed — this is an APIPA address
Someone set a static IP incorrectly

storyBeats.dialogue: Exactly right. 169.254.x.x always means DHCP failed. Check the relay, check the scope, check the server. Never accept it as normal. Fix it.
No. 169.254.0.0/16 is never a valid production address. It means DHCP failed and the host self-assigned. APIPA: Automatic Private IP Addressing. Always a symptom of a broken DHCP flow.
Not quite. 169.254.x.x is the APIPA range — auto-assigned by the OS when DHCP times out. A misconfigured static would be any other address. This specific range always means DHCP failure.

NAT translates private IPs to a public routable address. PAT (overload) maps many private IPs to one public IP using port numbers. Inside = your network, Outside = internet side. Router rewrites the IP header on the way out.

concept_visual.title: NAT & PAT Translation
concept_visual.explanation: Trace a packet as it crosses the NAT boundary. PAT maps hundreds of inside local addresses to one public IP using unique port numbers — step through each translation entry.

storyBeats.dialogue: NTP syncs clocks. Stratum 0 is the atomic clock reference. Stratum 1 syncs to stratum 0, stratum 2 syncs to stratum 1. Your routers should be stratum 3 or lower. Without NTP, log timestamps are garbage and security certs break.

Syslog has 8 levels: 0=Emergency, 1=Alert, 2=Critical, 3=Error, 4=Warning, 5=Notice, 6=Info, 7=Debug. Lower number = more severe. Production alert threshold is usually 3 or 4. Never run Debug in production — floods the buffer.

QoS marks traffic so high-priority packets (voice, video) aren’t dropped when links get congested. DSCP values 0-63, marked in the IP header. EF (46) for voice, AF for business data, BE (0) for default. Mark near the source.

Remember: 169.254=DHCP failure. DORA=Discover Offer Request Ack. Syslog 0=emergency 7=debug. NTP stratum lower=closer to source. Know the inside/outside NAT distinction — it trips people up in the lab.
Ticket #5512: 'Sales floor still can’t reach the internet. Also three branch routers are sending logs with wrong timestamps.' Ravi says these are your lab scenarios. Don’t break anything else.

Three labs: fix DHCP relay and scope on the sales VLAN, configure OSPF on the management segment, and set up PAT for the ISP edge. The hard one is the ISP edge — inside/outside NAT plus ACL interaction. Think before you type.

One warning: the ISP edge lab has a trick. The inside/outside NAT interfaces are labelled but the ip nat inside and ip nat outside commands are swapped. Find it before you test. 'show ip nat translations' is your friend.

Commands to know: 'show ip dhcp binding', 'show ip dhcp pool', 'show ip nat translations', 'show ip nat statistics'. Verify with a ping from the client after each fix. Don’t close the ticket until end-to-end connectivity confirms.

Sales is back online. But the ISP edge is throwing errors I’ve never seen — something’s consuming NAT translations faster than they’re timing out. The Services Hydra. It regenerates broken services as fast as you fix them.

Fix each service in order: DHCP, then NAT, then NTP synchronisation, then syslog routing. Each fix you make sticks. If you fix them out of order, dependencies fail. Methodical wins. Also — Priya is watching. Don’t make her come down here.

Hydra’s down. All services green. I’ll be honest — you’re better at this than I expected. One thing: Priya’s been watching your access logs. She wants to talk. That’s either good or very bad. Good luck.

choices.text: What should I know about Priya?

storyBeats.dialogue: She tests everyone. Cold open, a question you don’t expect. Don’t bluff. If you don’t know something, say so. She respects that more than a wrong confident answer. And don’t touch anything in the SOC without permission.

You’re the junior Maya vouched for. I don’t care about vouching. I care about whether you make the network less safe. There’s a rogue DHCP server on the guest VLAN. Marcus already has three tickets on it. Start talking.

choices.text: What do you need me to do?

storyBeats.dialogue: Rogue DHCP on VLAN 40. It’s handing out wrong gateway IPs — classic MITM setup. Every host that gets an address from it is compromised. We need DHCP snooping and DAI live on every access switch in that VLAN. Today.

choices.text: What are ACLs and why do I need them?

storyBeats.dialogue: Understand the taxonomy. Threat: any potential harm. Vulnerability: a weakness that can be exploited. Exploit: the specific action taken. Malware: virus (replicates via files), worm (self-propagates across networks), ransomware (encrypts data, demands payment), trojan (disguised as legitimate software). Attacks: phishing (fake messages to steal credentials), spear phishing (targeted), DoS (single-source resource exhaustion), DDoS (multi-source, botnet-driven), MITM (intercepts and alters communications). Social engineering bypasses every technical control by targeting people. Memorise the definitions — the exam will give you a scenario and ask you to identify the attack type.

AAA: Authentication — who are you? Authorization — what can you do? Accounting — what did you do? Local databases don’t scale. Centralise with a AAA server. RADIUS: UDP ports 1812 and 1813, encrypts only the password field. Used for network access: VPN, wireless 802.1X. TACACS+: TCP port 49, encrypts the entire payload, separates authentication and authorization. Preferred for device administration because it logs every individual command. Cisco ISE is the common AAA server. Exam question: which encrypts the full payload? TACACS+. Which uses UDP? RADIUS. Which is open standard? RADIUS.

choices.text: Tell me about the ACL approach.

concept_visual.title: AAA: RADIUS vs TACACS+
concept_visual.explanation: Step through Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting flows. See exactly which ports each protocol uses, what it encrypts, and why TACACS+ is preferred for device administration.

storyBeats.dialogue: Weak passwords are the single most exploited vulnerability. Policy requirements: minimum 12 characters, mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols. Passphrases — four or more random words — are long, memorable, and harder to crack than short complex strings. Set expiry so stale credentials don’t linger. Prohibit reuse. On IOS: 'security passwords min-length 12'. Always use 'enable secret' — it hashes with MD5. Never use 'enable password' — it’s cleartext. 'service password-encryption' applies weak Type 7 obfuscation to line passwords — not encryption. For remote access, SSH only: 'crypto key generate rsa modulus 2048', 'ip ssh version 2', line vty with 'transport input ssh'. Disable Telnet in production always.

ACLs are ordered lists of permit/deny statements. The router checks each packet top-down, first match wins. Every ACL has an implicit deny all at the end. If you forget that, you’ll block everything and wonder why.

concept_visual.title: ACL Permit / Deny Logic
concept_visual.explanation: Build an ACL and run test traffic through it. Packets are matched top-down — first match wins. The implicit deny at the end silently drops anything that does not match a rule above it.

storyBeats.dialogue: Standard ACLs match source IP only. Place them near the destination or you risk blocking too much. Extended ACLs match source, destination, protocol, and port. Place them near the source to stop traffic early. Placement is not optional knowledge.
You need to block traffic from 10.0.1.0/24 to a specific web server on port 443. Extended or standard ACL?

choices.text: Extended — match src, dst, and port

storyBeats.dialogue: Correct. Extended ACL. You need to match destination IP and port 443 — standard can’t do that. Place it on the interface closest to 10.0.1.0/24. And don’t forget the implicit deny at the end of every ACL.

Wrong. Standard ACLs can only match source IP. They can’t specify destination or port 443. You’d block all traffic from that subnet to everywhere. Extended ACL is required here. Place it near the source.

They do not work the same way. Standard ACLs match source only. You cannot specify a destination server or port 443 with a standard ACL. Extended is required. This distinction appears on the exam and in every real security review.

Good instinct. Framework: define what must be permitted first, deny everything else. Test in a maintenance window. Always have a rollback. Verify with 'show access-lists' hit counters. Blocking is only safe if you know what you’re blocking.

DHCP snooping separates trusted from untrusted ports. Trusted ports: your known DHCP servers. Untrusted: everything else. The switch drops DHCP Offer or Ack messages arriving on untrusted ports. Rogue DHCP server? Neutralised.

Dynamic ARP Inspection validates ARP packets against the DHCP snooping binding table. If a host claims an IP/MAC pair that doesn’t match a known lease, the ARP is dropped. Stops ARP spoofing. DAI requires DHCP snooping to be active first.

Port security limits which MAC addresses can use a switch port. Set a maximum MAC count. Violation modes: shutdown (port disabled, default), restrict (drops and logs), protect (drops silently). Shutdown is safest for user access ports.

VPNs create encrypted tunnels over untrusted networks. Site-to-site VPN: two routers negotiate a permanent IPsec tunnel connecting two offices. Remote access VPN: individual users connect on demand. Cisco AnyConnect uses SSL/TLS. IPsec protocol choices: AH (Authentication Header) provides integrity and source authentication but no encryption — the payload is visible. ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) provides both integrity and encryption — this is what you actually deploy. Two phases: IKE Phase 1 establishes the management tunnel (ISAKMP SA), Phase 2 establishes the data tunnel (IPsec SA). Exam: AH vs ESP — which encrypts? ESP. Site-to-site vs remote access — know both use cases.

Know ACL placement rules cold: standard near destination, extended near source. Memorise implicit deny. Know DHCP snooping trusted/untrusted and that DAI depends on it. Port security violation modes. These are guaranteed exam questions.
Tickets #6001 through #6009: 'Rogue DHCP still active', 'Can’t SSH to core switch', 'Guest VLAN routing to corp network'. Priya says these are your labs. Six scenarios. She’ll be watching the logs.

Six labs: management hardening, full device hardening, standard ACL, extended ACL, VTY access control, and OSPF authentication. The hard ones are full hardening and OSPF auth — every command matters. Wrong sequence = failed lab.

choices.text: Any tips for the hard labs?

storyBeats.dialogue: Enable DHCP snooping globally before configuring DAI. Set trusted ports before turning on inspection or you lock yourself out. For VTY ACL: apply it with access-class, not ip access-group. The distinction matters.

Verify with: 'show ip dhcp snooping binding', 'show ip arp inspection statistics', 'show port-security interface', 'show access-lists'. After hardening: try SSH from an unauthorised host — it should fail. That’s how you know it works.

Rogue DHCP is isolated. All snooping tables are clean. But the IDS just flagged 40,000 ARP packets in 60 seconds from a single port. Something is still active. The ACL Guardian. It’s been here before we arrived.

A policy engine running adversarial permit rules — it undoes your ACL changes in real time. You need to write and apply ACLs faster than it can corrupt them, then lock down the management plane so it can’t get back in.

Guardian is contained. Management plane is clean. You’re methodical. I can work with methodical. Don’t get comfortable — but you’ve earned access to the SOC logs. Maya wants a word before you go up to Chen.

Priya doesn’t say that to everyone. She said it to me once, six months after I started. Chen wants to see you in the Executive Briefing Room. He doesn’t do that unless he’s impressed. Go. Don’t be late.
Sit down. Tell me what you know about REST APIs. Don’t look at your notes.

choices.text: They use HTTP methods — GET, POST, PUT, DELETE — to interact with resources via URLs

storyBeats.dialogue: Good. That’s the northbound API. Now: what’s on the southbound side of an SDN controller?

choices.text: OpenFlow, NETCONF — protocols that program the data plane

storyBeats.dialogue: Honesty. I can work with that. REST APIs use HTTP methods — GET, POST, PUT, DELETE — to read or modify resources identified by URLs. JSON is the common data format. Cisco DNA Center uses REST northbound. Learn it.

Not wrong. Not complete. REST APIs use HTTP to expose network resources programmatically. GET to read, POST to create, PUT to update, DELETE to remove. In networking: DNA Center exposes REST APIs to manage devices at scale.

Traditional networking: control plane and data plane live on the same device. SDN separates them. A centralised controller handles routing decisions — the control plane. Switches just forward — the data plane. Northbound to apps, southbound to devices.

choices.text: What protocols are on the southbound?

concept_visual.title: SDN & Cloud Architecture
concept_visual.explanation: See how SDN separates the control plane from the data plane. A centralised controller programs forwarding decisions via southbound APIs (OpenFlow / NETCONF) while switches just forward.

storyBeats.dialogue: OpenFlow programs the forwarding table directly. NETCONF uses SSH over port 830 to send configuration in XML. RESTCONF is NETCONF concepts over HTTP. Each has its place. NETCONF is the standard for Cisco IOS XE configuration management.

YANG is a data modelling language. It defines the structure of configuration and operational data — what fields exist, their types, constraints. NETCONF and RESTCONF use YANG models to validate data. Think of it as a schema for device configuration.

Ansible is agentless — no software on the device, just SSH. Playbooks are YAML files defining tasks. The ios_config module pushes IOS commands. Idempotent: run it 100 times, same result. This replaces manual CLI for large deployments.

Know the automation tools. Ansible: agentless, no software required on the device. Uses SSH. YAML playbooks. Idempotent — running a playbook twice produces no additional changes if state is already correct. No master server. Puppet: agent-based — an agent runs on every device and checks in with the Puppet master. Declarative model: describe desired state, Puppet reconciles. Chef: also agent-based, procedural model — recipes describe steps to reach desired state. For the CCNA exam: agentless means Ansible. Agent-based means Puppet or Chef. And if port 22 is refused, Ansible cannot connect because it relies on SSH.
An Ansible playbook fails with 'connection refused' on port 22. What is the likely issue?

choices.text: SSH not enabled on the device

storyBeats.dialogue: Correct. Ansible uses SSH by default. Port 22 refused means SSH is not enabled or is blocked. On IOS: 'ip ssh version 2' and 'line vty 0 4 / transport input ssh'. Always verify SSH before running a playbook against a new device.

choices.text: Tell me about Python for networking

storyBeats.dialogue: YANG errors look different — usually a model validation failure, not a connection refused on port 22. Port 22 is SSH. Ansible is agentless and uses SSH to reach devices. Connection refused on 22 means SSH is not running on the target.

choices.text: Tell me about Python for networking

storyBeats.dialogue: REST APIs run over HTTP/HTTPS on ports 80/443, not port 22. Port 22 is SSH. Ansible’s default connection method is SSH. If port 22 is refused, SSH is not enabled or is firewalled on the target device.

choices.text: Tell me about Python for networking

storyBeats.dialogue: Python’s requests library sends HTTP calls to REST APIs. json.loads() parses JSON responses into Python dicts. json.dumps() serialises back to JSON. This is how you automate DNA Center: authenticate, get a token, pass it in headers for every call.

REST APIs return JSON. NETCONF uses XML. Know both on sight. Valid JSON: curly braces for objects, square brackets for arrays, keys and string values in double quotes, numbers and booleans unquoted. Example: {"hostname": "R1", "interfaces": [{"name": "Gi0/0", "ip": "10.0.0.1"}]}. Invalid JSON: single quotes, trailing commas, missing braces. Valid XML: opening and closing tags, attributes in quotes, one root element. Example: <config><hostname>R1</hostname></config>. The exam may show you a data block and ask which format it is, or whether the JSON is syntactically valid. If you see curly braces and colons, it’s JSON. If you see angle-bracket tags, it’s XML.

Cisco DNA Center — now Catalyst Center — is a controller-based network management platform. It abstracts device configuration into intent-based policies. REST API northbound. NETCONF/RESTCONF southbound. Replaces per-device CLI management at scale.

Know: SDN separates control and data plane. NETCONF=SSH/830, RESTCONF=HTTP. YANG=data model. Ansible=agentless/YAML/SSH. REST uses GET/POST/PUT/DELETE. DNA Center uses northbound REST. These distinctions appear on every practice exam.

Chen asked me to introduce the capstone. Two labs: a guided automation scenario and a full capstone that combines everything — routing, services, security, and automation. This is the final exam before the boss. Take your time.

Basic lab: write an Ansible playbook using ios_config, call a REST API with Python requests, and verify with NETCONF. Advanced: configure a full topology — OSPF, DHCP relay, ACLs, port security, then automate a change via DNA Center API.

choices.text: Any warnings for the advanced lab?

storyBeats.dialogue: The advanced lab grades you on idempotency — run your playbook twice, the second run should make zero changes. If it’s not idempotent, find out why. Also: authenticate to the REST API first and cache the token. Don’t re-auth on every call.

Verify: Ansible runs with --check first (dry run). Python script prints status codes — 200=OK, 401=auth failed, 404=wrong endpoint. 'show running-config' confirms device state matches playbook intent. Test from a clean state each time.

Capstone passed. Every system is green. But the automation layer just flagged an entity rewriting API responses — injecting bad configs into the pipeline. The Automation Oracle. It’s been learning from every change you’ve made.

It exploits every misconfiguration in your automation stack. Validate your YANG models, verify API authentication is correct, confirm Ansible playbooks are idempotent. Lock down the northbound API access. Then push the correcting playbook.

Oracle neutralised. Every system nominal. Six weeks. You came in not knowing a VLAN from a routing table. You leave knowing how to build and automate an enterprise network. The team wants to say something.

Maya: 'Don’t put a wrong network statement in OSPF.' Ravi: 'Check the relay first. Always.' Priya: 'You’re methodical. Don’t lose that.' Marcus: 'Please. No more tickets.' Chen: 'You’re ready for the exam. Go pass it.'
